caveats: a: The occupation source covers a different four-digit machinery subsector and is therefore only a labor-mix proxy.
a: The mapping estimates current unautomated task exposure rather than observed AI deployment, and eligible service-rich firms may differ from the industry average.
rho: DOE establishes manufacturing use cases and technical feasibility rather than adoption rates in NAICS 333413.
rho: Small-firm software maturity, product-liability controls, customer data access, and legacy equipment constraints are not measured directly.
e: The assigned firm-count input is a margin-bridged estimate rather than an observed count of eligible firms.
e: Official industry scope does not identify recurring revenue, earnings, ownership, or transferability.
s5: This is bounded judgment without an observed industry-specific transfer rate.
s5: The estimated firm denominator and sparse disclosure of small private transactions weaken calibration.
q: No source directly measures commercial retention of AI-enabled benefits in this industry.
q: Retention is likely higher for proprietary retrofits, controls, and service than for competitively specified standard equipment.
d5: The BLS output series combines several machinery industries and is not specific to NAICS 333413.
d5: Real output is an imperfect quantity measure and can reflect mix and quality changes.

d5: DOE currently reports no federal energy conservation standards for fans and blowers after withdrawal of the proposal, so federal efficiency regulation is not treated as a guaranteed replacement-cycle catalyst.
o: The estimate applies after the quantity change in d5 and avoids counting demand loss twice.
o: Some monitoring and controls may shift to building-management platforms or larger integrated OEMs, but the physical air-handling function remains.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Industrial and Commercial Fan and Blower and Air Purification Equipment Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include industrial plants, commercial buildings, institutions, contractors, and equipment integrators. Revenue can combine engineered equipment orders with repeat replacement projects, filters and wear parts, controls, upgrades, field service, inspection, commissioning, and technical support tied to an installed base.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure one-off product sellers without repeat customer work, household-type fans and portable air purifiers assigned to other industries, air-conditioning equipment manufacturers, captive internal units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US firms in NAICS 333413 that supply recurring or repeat outsourced work to external customers through engineered fan, blower, dust-collection, filtration, or stationary air-purification equipment programs and associated parts, filters, controls, upgrades, maintenance, diagnostics, or technical support, within the lower-middle-market band.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations and wages for NAICS 333300 commercial and service machinery manufacturing, not task exposure for NAICS 333413. I mapped management, business, engineering, sales, office, inspection, programming, planning, and diagnostic tasks to partial generative-AI exposure and assigned little exposure to hands-on fabrication, assembly, installation, and repair, with qualitative wage weighting. The proxy could overstate exposure if fan and purification manufacturing is more production-intensive, or understate it if eligible firms carry more engineering and aftermarket support staff.
caveats: The occupation source covers a different four-digit machinery subsector and is therefore only a labor-mix proxy.
The mapping estimates current unautomated task exposure rather than observed AI deployment, and eligible service-rich firms may differ from the industry average.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory using payroll, time-study, and workflow data from representative NAICS 333413 manufacturers, separated into factory, engineering, sales, office, and aftermarket roles, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: The comparison machinery subsector has substantial knowledge work but remains physically intensive: production occupations represent 41.26% of employment, while management and business occupations represent 10.30% and 7.64%. AI can assist engineering documentation, quoting, configuration, procurement, scheduling, service knowledge, inspection, diagnostics, and customer support, but does not directly replace most fabrication, assembly, installation, or field repair.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for a combined machinery group containing NAICS 3334 to grow at 1.0% annually through 2034, not constant-quality demand for NAICS 333413. I converted that annual rate into a five-year ratio and widened the range for construction and industrial cyclicality, indoor-air-quality investment, and legally required exhaust systems. The broad proxy may understate this industry if filtration and ventilation upgrades outgrow machinery overall, or overstate it if nonresidential projects weaken or equipment life extends.
caveats: The BLS output series combines several machinery industries and is not specific to NAICS 333413.
Real output is an imperfect quantity measure and can reflect mix and quality changes.

DOE currently reports no federal energy conservation standards for fans and blowers after withdrawal of the proposal, so federal efficiency regulation is not treated as a guaranteed replacement-cycle catalyst.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-quality shipment volumes, order backlogs, filter and parts consumption, installed-base replacement rates, nonresidential project forecasts, and product-level forecasts for fans, blowers, and dust-collection systems would materially tighten this ratio.
d5.rationale: The broad machinery forecast implies modest real growth. EPA guidance treats ventilation, filtration, and supplemental air cleaning as core indoor-air-quality practices, while OSHA requires appropriately designed and maintained exhaust and dust-control systems in covered operations. These recurring physical needs support demand, though long equipment lives and cyclical capital spending limit confidence.

caveats: The assigned firm-count input is a margin-bridged estimate rather than an observed count of eligible firms.
Official industry scope does not identify recurring revenue, earnings, ownership, or transferability.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census combining NAICS validation, normalized EBITDA, equipment versus aftermarket revenue, installed-base size, service staffing, customer concentration, and control ownership would determine the eligible share.
e.rationale: Dust collectors, filters, controls, fans, and blowers create repeat replacement, retrofit, parts, and support needs, making a material subset eligible. Pure equipment vendors selling through distributors, project-only manufacturers, firms without transferable customer relationships, and businesses outside the target earnings band remain ineligible.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 333413 ($2,291,621,526) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 333413 ($8,103,578,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $283,530 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 333413); class <5: 87 firms, avg revenue/firm $896,540 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $175,901 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 58 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,025,276 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $397,359 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 47 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,079,340 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $604,167 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 121 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,601,702 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,668,654 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 42 firms, avg revenue/firm $34,143,048 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,698,866 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 27 firms, avg revenue/firm $173,465,556 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $34,033,942 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 163

caveats: The estimate applies after the quantity change in d5 and avoids counting demand loss twice.
Some monitoring and controls may shift to building-management platforms or larger integrated OEMs, but the physical air-handling function remains.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and product forecasts separating equipment and field-service demand from software-only monitoring, self-service maintenance, and integrated-system substitution would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Year-five demand remains overwhelmingly physical: moving, exhausting, filtering, or cleaning air requires equipment, installation compatibility, performance accountability, parts, and maintenance. Software can optimize controls, monitoring, and diagnostics but cannot replace the underlying fans, blowers, filters, collectors, or accountable supplier for most retained demand.

caveats: No source directly measures commercial retention of AI-enabled benefits in this industry.
Retention is likely higher for proprietary retrofits, controls, and service than for competitively specified standard equipment.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, bid histories, distributor terms, renewal pricing, warranty obligations, and gross-margin changes following productivity initiatives would materially change the retained-benefit estimate.
q.rationale: Engineered performance, application knowledge, installed-base compatibility, safety, efficiency representations, and rapid service support can protect part of productivity gains. Competitive equipment bids, contractor and distributor bargaining, customer procurement, renewal repricing, and transparent component costs pass some benefits through.

caveats: DOE establishes manufacturing use cases and technical feasibility rather than adoption rates in NAICS 333413.
Small-firm software maturity, product-liability controls, customer data access, and legacy equipment constraints are not measured directly.
rho.change_evidence: A survey of eligible manufacturers documenting live AI use cases, deployment timing, integration cost, exception rates, validated labor hours, and production or service outcomes would materially move this range.
rho.rationale: Administrative, commercial, documentation, and support use cases can be implemented with ordinary workflow controls. Automated visual inspection, configuration engineering, predictive maintenance, and connected-product diagnostics require clean technical data, legacy-system integration, cybersecurity, sensor coverage, testing, and accountable human review, slowing implementation.

caveats: This is bounded judgment without an observed industry-specific transfer rate.
The estimated firm denominator and sparse disclosure of small private transactions weaken calibration.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, or BizBuySell matched to NAICS 333413, combined with owner-age and succession surveys of eligible firms, would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Privately held specialty manufacturers face ordinary succession and consolidation pressures, but no six-digit owner-age or completed-control-transfer series was found. Strategic sales, sponsor transactions, and family succession can qualify, while internal reorganizations and closures without transferable operations do not.

principal_driver: Installed-base parts, filters, retrofits, and technical support combine persistent physical air-quality needs with AI-addressable engineering and service workflows.
principal_weakness: Physical production constrains labor substitution, while project cyclicality and weak firm-level transfer evidence make realizable opportunity uncertain.
